data.title: Voyager Technologies: Back To Earth
data.description:
Early in September, I concluded that shares of Voyager Technologies (VOYG) were coming back to earth after shares rocketed higher to levels around the $50 mark when the business went public in June, in what easily can be called a hyped offering.

High sales multiples, choppy fundamentals, and big losses made me very cautious, especially as there were few signs of accelerating growth and/or an expanding backlog. In between shares falling to the $30 mark, valuation multiples came down, yet the same issues discussed above made me cautious to get involved for the same reasons.

Sadly I have to draw that conclusion again today, at just $18 per share, although the overall sales multiples do start to provide some support here.

A Space Business

Voyager was founded in 2019 as a defense technology and space solutions business, based on a mission of innovation, purposely built to deliver on such solutions. A focus on innovation, low-capital intensity, and growing end markets makes for a compelling situation, at least, on paper.

The company acts both in the role of a prime contractor, in which it provides integrated solutions to its customers, and as a merchant supplier as well. In this role, it is a subcontractor of key technologies for the likes of Lockheed Martin, NASA, and even Palantir.

The company reports its results across three divisions: Defense & National Security, Space Solutions, and Starlab Space Stations. Products, services, and capabilities provided include propulsion technologies, navigation, controls, intelligence, and a wide range of services and software solutions. The Starlab business is a JV with various stakeholders, designed to succeed the ISS by 2030, this being somewhat of an outlier or wild card in the business.

The company went public at $31 per share, levels at which operating assets were valued at $1.4 billion. This was applied to a business, which generated $144 million in sales in 2024, a number that rose by just eight million over the past year. Operating losses of $48 million were substantial, although in all fairness, in part driven by $12 million in impairment and amortization costs.

First quarter sales rose by 14% to $35 million, yet operating losses doubled to $26 million, these being hardly encouraging trends. Trading around $55 on the first day of trading, operating asset valuations jumped to $2.7 billion, as a 20 times annualised sales multiple was very demanding.

By September, shares had fallen to the $30 mark, as the company reported a 25% increase in second-quarter sales to $45 million and change, as operating losses were reported at $24 million. Notably, defense & national security sales rose sharply, offset by falling space solutions revenues. The company guided for full-year sales around $165-$170 million, which implied an $85-$90 million revenue outlook for the second half of 2025, at par with the annualised second-quarter sales results, hardly creating a compelling outlook.

Moreover, following a $9 million EBITDA loss reported in the second quarter, the implied second-half EBITDA loss guidance between $30 million and $33 million was rather disappointing, suggesting declining margins on the back of flattish sales. Back to a $1.4 billion operating asset valuation, the sales multiple declined to 8 times, yet lack of growth and large losses made me very cautious, even as some air has come out of the valuation of the business.

Coming Down Further

Since September, shares of Voyager have fallen by another third to $18, trading right around the 52-week lows. In October, Voyager announced the purchase of ExoTerra, in order to boost its propulsion capabilities, with no financial details on the purchase being announced.

Early in November, Voyager reported quarterly sales at $39.6 million, dead flat compared to the third quarter of 2024, and down from the second quarter number reported. GAAP operating losses of $24 million showed no signs of improvement here, with net cash holdings down to $413 million. The backlog was reported at $189 million, increasing by nearly ten million following a 1.25 times book-to-bill ratio reported.

By now the 58 million shares have fallen to the $18 mark, reducing the market value to $1.04 billion, for an operating asset valuation around $630 million, down substantially from the peak at around $3 billion.

The company guided for full-year sales at the high end of the $165-$170 million guidance, with adjusted EBITDA losses seen between $60 and $63 million. If revenues come in at the high end of the guidance, fourth-quarter sales will come in close to $50 million, with fourth-quarter adjusted EBITDA losses seen between $12 million and $15 million, an improvement from a $17.7 million loss in the third quarter.

Soon after the release of the results, the company issued a big convertible issue, which is noteworthy given the substantial net cash holdings reported. The company issued $435 million in convertible bonds, carrying coupons of just 0.75% due in 2030, with conversion set just a couple of pennies short of $31 per share, levels, which coincidentally are the same as the IPO price.

And Now?

With the operating asset valuation down to just over $600 million, sales multiples have collapsed from 20 times sales soon after the offering to just about 3 times here based on the annualised fourth quarter sales results. Of course, these are loss-making sales, with structural growth still hard to be found here.

While this is compelling on the one hand, and improvements in the business might be seen in 2026, I am puzzled at the same time about recent actions taken. With that I refer to the convertible bond issue, as conversion of the recent bond issue could involve the issuance of about 14 million shares, equal to a quarter of the current share base outstanding. Moreover, this might take place at levels just half of the highs seen over the summer, soon after the public offering.

Many risks continue to be present, including that of competition, reliance on a small customer base, reliance on government spending, technical issues, etc.

In between all this, I see appeal improving, yet continued losses and these capital allocation moves (the convertible issuance) are what make me very cautious here and still not be on the ride here.

data.title: Carrier Global: It's Decent But Not So Special As It Seems
data.description:
Amid the skyrocketing home prices and macroeconomic uncertainty, the residential property market has already slowed down from its boom in the past few years. With less demand and sales, HVAC systems for residential properties receive negative spillovers. Even a popular brand like Carrier Global Corporation (CARR) experiences the same trend, which squeezes its growth and margins. So, I think the stock price downtrend is justifiable, supported by the fact that it’s still fully valued. Technicals adhere to them as bearish signals remain prominent despite the recent overselling.

CARR Q3 2025: Market Weakness Was Evident

Stubborn inflation and residential market softness influence the performance of many HVAC companies. After all, their demand is also correlated with home sales and inventory. This trend is still evident in Carrier Global Corporation, as its performance remained soft.

During Q3 2025, its total sales amounted to $5.6B, down by 6.7% YoY from $6.0B. Both its product and service segments weakened as HVAC demand continued to soften. If we look at it geographically, it had its weakest performance in the Americas, as segment sales there dropped by 8.4% YoY.

CARR may have also contracted its operating capacity to keep up with the rising prices and manage cost pressures. And I believe that worked somehow. After all, its total costs and expenses decreased. To be more specific, the portion of its COGS to the total costs and expenses decreased a bit from 81.6% to 81.3%. This means that CARR still managed to lower its cost sensitivity despite inflation. This was not enough to offset the revenue decrease because the operating margin decreased from 12.8% to 9.7%. But it helped the company maintain positive profits.

Risks

CARR faces various challenges today. One of these is stubborn inflation. In the US, inflation has already picked up momentum and rebounded to its 14-month high of 3.0% in only a few months. New tariffs can aggravate the situation due to their inflationary nature. Although CARR has its mitigation strategies and supply chain efficiencies, the overall impact on the economy must be considered. The rising prices may affect purchasing power.

Another challenge is the residential market softness. The median home sales price in the US remains elevated at $410,800. Although it has been decreasing since Q4 2022 at $442,600, the price is still much higher than pre-pandemic levels. With that, it’s not surprising that vacancy rates have increased since 2021.

We must also note the huge reduction in existing home sales in recent years. This proves the continued weakness in demand as home prices remain skyrocketing. Considering this, we may expect residential HVAC sales to stay subdued and muted for an extended period.

Now, you may be wondering if this will lead to a reset or a bubble burst. But I believe that this is still highly unlikely because property builders have already learned their lesson from the Great Recession. You can see their conservative approach to growth and response to slowing home demand. Home inventory has never bounced back to GFC levels. You can also see that multi-family home construction and starts also dropped in the same year when home demand decreased. This trend will prevent home prices from crashing because a lower supply can partially offset lower demand. If you go back to the median home sales price trend, the value has decreased in the past 14 quarters but at a slow and controlled rate. This allows buyers and property builders to have some breathing room and adjust to prevailing market dynamics.

Despite this, I don’t expect a rebound in the residential property market anytime soon. Even CARR may not expect growth in this segment as economic conditions remain bleak.

Risk Mitigants

Despite these challenges, I believe that CARR is not fighting a losing game. It has some insulation against various headwinds and still promises longevity.

One aspect to consider is its supply chain management to lower tariff and geographic risks. According to its annual report, the company uses various raw materials, supplier-provided parts, and third-party service providers across the globe. This gives it some flexibility to adjust its sourcing or importing of raw materials, parts, and services. On top of that, CARR does dual sourcing and regionalization to mitigate supply chain risks and tariff exposure. This is supported by its significant domestic footprint in other regions like Europe and the Asia Pacific.

Another core strength of CARR is its decent fundamentals. Because it is still viable, it also generates positive cash flow. In fact, its operating cash inflow is still enough to cover capex, dividends, and debt repayments. The problem is that the notable decrease in operating cash flow limited its capacity to cover share repurchases. So, it’s not surprising that its cash level has fallen in the past 11 quarters.

To ensure liquidity, CARR ensures prudent borrowing management. Although the amount remains very high at over $11B, its maturity profile remains long. In fact, almost 80% of the total amount has a maturity of five years and above. This is in line with its Net Debt/EBITDA of 2.8x, which means that CARR can cover all borrowings at once in less than three years even if it doesn’t have to. Hence, it still has enough capital buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Digital and AI Revolution May Help, but Not That Much

Many people may have already realized that HVAC companies like Carrier Global Corporation are essential to the digital and AI revolution. As more houses and businesses require larger internet access, data centers may have to expand further in the coming years. Statistics present that the global data center capacity demand may increase by 3.5x from 2025 to 2030.

This can be covered by the expanding data center inventory in the past two years. However, there is still some disconnect in some locations because vacancy rates remained higher in Q1 2025 than in Q1 2023. This can tell us that demand has increased faster than inventory. So, data center expansion may speed up.

With that, the HVAC industry may see an influx of demand because of its crucial role in data centers. They need HVAC to ensure effectiveness and safety due to the fact that servers may generate massive heat. For example, AI chips may need thermal management because of their tendency to generate massive heat per rack. With thermal management, data centers can maintain the required narrow temperature band. Climate control precision is also important since HVAC systems are there to regulate temperature and humidity and prevent condensation that may cause electrical shorts and corrosion. These can also lead to downtimes, which can be costly for data centers. Lastly, data centers may need HVAC to boost energy efficiency in cooling because they account for a big portion of energy consumption.

Despite this, I don’t expect CARR to benefit from this as much as other HVAC companies like Trane Technologies (TT) and Vertiv (VRT) will. Why? CARR is more exposed to the residential market and light-commercial than commercial and industrial markets. Its HVAC systems are mainly distributed to houses and smaller commercial establishments, which makes them quite unfit for data centers.

I understand that CARR is starting to position and lean into these opportunities. Yet, it may also face intense competition. Aside from TT and VRT, it may compete with Johnson Controls (JCI) and Daikin (OTCPK:DKILF). Note that the estimated data center chiller market share is still dominated by Johnson Controls with 24% and Trane Technologies with 20%. Carrier remains competitive with 17%. However, its more traditional chillers or air systems might be slower to adapt than its peers if AI data centers shift more toward novel cooling technologies.

In addition, CARR only has a 7% market share of thermal equipment. For computer room air handling, or CRAH, units, CARR is still off the radar, while VRT has 28%.

Overall, VRT remains the strongest pure play with its specialized exposure in the data center-specific market share. JCI comes second as it rapidly scales into high-density AI workloads. Trane is strong in chillers. Meanwhile, Daikin and CARR are less focused and have smaller market shares. To cope with the competition and innovation, CARR may have to build and customize its cooling systems, which can be capital-intensive. It may also have to wait for long deployment cycles as it tries to fit these new products into the data center market.

Valuation

CARR has already decreased noticeably in the past year. Yet, it doesn’t appear cheap for me to rush into it. As I looked into its financial metrics, I noticed that the stock price trend appears most consistent with sales. So, I used P/S to assess the stock price relative to sales. Currently, it trades at 2.10x its stock price, which is quite cheaper than the five-year average of 2.12x. If we use this to derive its fair value, the TP, or target price, will be $53.95. This means that CARR is trading at a reasonable level. This also means that the price downtrend is justifiable. However, the gap between the current and target price is narrow, so upside potential may be very limited. Indeed, CARR is still fully valued.

To have a more forward-looking approach, I also used the Dividend Discount Model. Its annualized dividend of $0.90 is based on its quarterly payout of $0.2250. It is also worth noting that I set its risk premium at 6.0% instead of the standard of 5.0% to price in more risks and be more conservative. Lastly, I estimated Beta on MS Excel using the SLOPE function of the daily price change versus the S&P 500. Using these numbers, its TP is $57.93. If I add a 5% risk consideration, the TP will be $55.03. Either way, CARR is still almost fully valued. Hence, I advise investors to wait for a better entry point.

Technicals

CARR’s technicals are bearish as the price keeps decreasing with lower highs and lows below the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that the downtrend still holds. It also means that the price trend or momentum is weaker than the average in the past 50 or 200 days. Meanwhile, the 50-day SMA is still trending downwards below the 200-day SMA, which shows that market sentiments are still pessimistic as the selloff continues.

Its MACD and RSI can prove it. The MACD line is still trending downwards below the signal line, which means that selling volume continues to increase. It also stays within negative territory along with the histogram, which implies that selling volume still outpaces buying volume. Lastly, RSI presents a weak trend even if CARR becomes oversold.

Conclusion

Carrier Global Corporation experiences softer market conditions that have squeezed its growth and margins recently. It is still well-positioned with its decent fundamentals and ample liquidity, but catalysts remain limited due to its high exposure to the residential segment. These things justify its recent price downtrend, supported by its valuation with low upside. Technicals conform with these aspects as bearish signals continue. I am giving a hold rating on this stock.

data.title: Madison Mid Cap Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The Russell Mid Cap Index total return accelerated in the third quarter of 2025. Information Technology was the top performing sector in both the quarter and year-to-date periods. After starting the year as the worst performing sector in the first quarter, two consecutive quarters with exceptionally strong returns helped the IT sector improve to the best performing sector in both the second and third quarters. The Utilities sector was the second-best performing sector in both the quarter and YTD periods. Both sectors are benefiting from widespread exuberance around AI and the increased use of momentum-based investment strategies that typically accompany such investor sentiment.

The Madison Mid Cap Fund (Class I) (MDCIX) increased 1.9% in the third quarter of 2025, compared to the 5.3% increase in the Russell Midcap Index.

Portfolio Performance

The top five contributors for the quarter were Medpace, Amphenol, Arista Networks, MKS, and Ross Stores. Shares in Medpace jumped following much better-than-expected 2Q results. Investors were expecting growth to continue to slow for the contract research industry, but instead, Medpace posted accelerating 2Q revenue and bookings growth, and expressed confidence in the trends continuing for the rest of the year.

Technology stocks continued their very strong performance in 3Q, and we participated with Amphenol, Arista Networks, and MKS within our portfolio. Amphenol and Arista continue to surprise investors with outstanding revenue growth as they capitalize on the surge in AI-related data center spending. While data center growth is a meaningful benefit, we are also encouraged by the strength in other areas of these businesses, such as enterprise for Arista and defense and industrial markets for Amphenol. MKS outperformed as investor sentiment continues to warm to an improving semiconductor cycle, as the company's results once again reflected end markets picking up.

Finally, investors found improved sales growth and management commentary surrounding tariff mitigation efforts encouraging at Ross Stores, sending the shares higher during the quarter.

The bottom five detractors for the quarter were Gartner, Liberty Broadband (LBRDA), Brown & Brown, Carlisle, and CDW. Shares in Gartner declined in the quarter following disappointing 2Q results. Growth slowed more than expected, which management attributed to the macro environment (companies, especially those impacted by tariffs, weren't in the mood to add to their expenses), while the market attributed the slowing to AI disruption (companies substituting generative AI tools for Gartner subscriptions). We believe the AI risk to Gartner is manageable, as the combination of their strong reputation, proprietary data, including surveys, models, benchmarking data, 500k annual client calls, 27k annual tech vendor calls, etc., creates a trove of unique insights you can't get with generative AI. Liberty Broadband, which derives nearly all its value from its ownership stake in Charter Communications (CHTR), dropped as Charter reported a disappointing quarter of profitability and broadband subscriber losses. In 1Q, investor sentiment rose following promising trends in these metrics, so this quarter marked a step back.

Organic growth at Brown & Brown slowed a bit faster than many investors anticipated, although it remains healthy and within historical norms. Cyclical headwinds within Carlisle's residential and commercial construction end markets weighed on the shares during the quarter. Finally, increased costs at CDW dampened profits, despite better-than-expected top line growth.

Portfolio Activity

We trimmed two stocks during the quarter, Arista Networks and MKS. As noted above, both of these stocks have performed well recently. While we continue to see strong prospects for both businesses, we decided to trim back our holdings to a lower weighting, given these stocks' higher valuations.

We sold one holding, GCI Liberty (GCI), which was spun out of Liberty Broadband during the quarter. GCI Liberty owns cable and telecom assets in Alaska. Given mediocre prospects for growth, as well as the small market capitalization of the business and immaterial position in our portfolio, we sold the shares shortly after receiving them.

Outlook and Positioning

As always, we continue our search for quality companies with shareholder-oriented management teams selling below our assessments of intrinsic value. We are invested in a variety of high-quality, durable businesses in a wide range of industries, selling for reasonable, or even cheap, prices. This has historically been a formula for solid, long-term performance. We don't think that has changed. But recently, these attributes have been unpopular, as the market has favored the short-term over the long-term, the promise versus the proven, and momentum over durability. While we dedicate a considerable amount of time to understanding new technologies and using them ourselves we will not risk our investors' hard-earned savings by abandoning a philosophy that makes rational, clear-headed sense to us, and that has worked for decades, in order to conform to something that may be fleeting, and appears to be dependent on highly optimistic scenarios. Our style of companies may temporarily be out of favor, but we believe our all-weather portfolio remains well-positioned to deliver attractive risk adjusted returns in a variety of market environments.

Respectfully,

Haruki Toyama

Andy Romanowich

Rich Eisinger

data.title: Suburban Propane: 2025 Growth Is Likely An Aberration
data.description:
Shares of Suburban Propane (SPH) have been a moderate performer over the past year, gaining 4% and paying out a 7% distribution. A colder winter helped support results this year, but I would be hesitant to extrapolate that trend forward. While it pays a sizable distribution, long-term growth prospects are challenged, and its balance sheet has sizable debt. I last covered shares of Suburban Propane in May, rating the stock a “sell,” and since then, shares are basically unchanged, missing out on a 17% market rally, justifying my negative view.

In the partnership's fiscal fourth quarter, Suburban Propane lost $0.53, which was still a penny better than expected, as revenue grew 1% to $211 million. The company generated $1 million of EBITDA, flat to last year. Of course, the propane business is highly seasonal, with demand substantially higher during the winter months as homes in the North turn on the heat. As such, Q4 results tend to be seasonally weak, and its fiscal H1 is much more important to results than H2.

Still, in Q4, retail volumes were 61 million gallons, up 1.8% from last year. That said, operating costs were up 3.4% from last year to $114 million, while G&A was up over 10% to $21 million. This outpaced revenue and volume growth, which may pressure margins somewhat next year. In fiscal 2025, propane volumes were up 6%, thanks to a colder winter, and EBITDA grew 11% to $278 million. Back in May, I was targeting $275-285 million in EBITDA and $120 million of free cash flow, so results were within my expectations.

While propane volumes were higher in 2025, I would be hesitant to extrapolate this as a trend. Temperatures were 4% cooler than last year, with its key months of January and February 13% colder. As you can see below, propane volumes hit a 15-year high in January given a severe cold spell (if you recall, it even snowed in New Orleans and Florida). We could have an equally cold winter this year—it is too soon to know. Still, as you can see below, volumes have been slowly but steadily marching lower over time. Gas utilities continue to build out networks, gradually replacing propane as a fuel source in the winter heating season. Plus, to the extent temperatures are rising, this provides a small but structural pressure on heating demand.

Now thinking about fiscal 2026, we have seen an unusual cold spell in November, which may help to boost Q1 results. Frankly, this cold spell was needed just to avoid starting the year on a poor footing. Inventory levels typically peak ahead of the winter demand season, but as you can see below, inventories are reaching new highs. While last year’s severe cold spell brought inventories to relatively low levels, that has been entirely recovered, pointing to weaker demand of late.

That is partly because this year there was an extremely quiet hurricane season, and there were no hurricanes that made landfall on the mainland US. This is a negative for Suburban, though hurricane season is not as important as the winter. Hurricanes can lead to power outages, and many generators in Florida run on propane, creating a source of demand, given SPH’s presence in the state. With no power outages, Florida households and businesses likely still have higher levels of propane in their tanks at the end of hurricane season. This may reduce refilling needs in fiscal 2026 ahead of the next hurricane season to some degree. While a headwind, it is relatively modest, with winter demand from the Northeast a much larger driver of results. On net, the colder November weather likely offsets this lost demand, with SPH starting fiscal 2026 on a neutral footing.

Given the reliance on weather for demand trends, SPH does not provide revenue guidance at this point, but it did provide some spending guidance. Notably, Suburban expects to spend about $75 million on capex next year. For context, it spent $14 million on cap-ex in Q4, down from $19 million last year as it cut back on growth spending. For the full year, it invested $72 million in cap-ex.

Aside from cap-ex, SPH does continue to engage in tuck-in M&A. In October, SPH spent $24 million to add two distribution businesses in California. SPH partially funds these activities with share sales. During 2025, it sold $24 million in equity. Due to equity issuance, its share count is up 2.2% from last year. The fact that SPH continues to issue shares as part of its $100 million at-the-market program speaks to the fact that management does not view SPH stock as cheap.

Plus, given its debt load, it needs some equity funding to support bolt-on deals. SPH ended the quarter with 4.3x leverage, down from 4.8x a year ago. This is primarily due to the recovery in EBITDA amid colder weather. Given its lack of long-term, I view it as important to bring leverage down to a more sustainable 4x, and I expect ongoing equity issuance as a result.

For this year, I am expecting $260-280 million of EBITDA, or about $190 million of operating cash flow. This assumes winter weather that is relatively normal. Given its cap-ex plans, SPH should generate about $120 million of free cash flow again in 2026. This translates to about $1.80 per share. That gives SPH a just-under 10% free cash flow yield. That provides a solid 1.4x coverage ratio and leaves just over ~$30 million of debt reduction capacity, or about a ~0.1x reduction in leverage. This positions the partnership to get back to ~4x leverage by the end of 2028.

Back in May, I saw Suburban as unlikely to increase its distribution for 2-4 years as it focuses on reducing debt. On top of this, I saw long-term secular declines of 0-2%, and this drove my $15.50-$16.50 price target. I expect the growth we saw in 2025 to prove the exception and not the start of the new trend. A sub-10% free cash flow yield, especially when excess cash flow will go to debt reduction for several years, does not compensate investors for the risk of sustained long-term pressure, and investors should want an 8+% dividend yield, not a 7% dividend yield, given the lack of potential growth.

At this valuation, I see no clear upside catalyst, as evidenced by the fact SPH has been unable to participate in a meaningful market rally over the past six months. I still believe fair value is closer to an 11-12% free cash flow yield to protect against modest ongoing declines. I retain my $16 price target and remain a sell on SPH given this downside risk.

data.title: TCW Artificial Intelligence ETF Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Thesis

We believe that the artificial intelligence transformation is in the very early stages, with massive potential for use cases that will have an impact on nearly every sector and all aspects of our daily lives, from driving a car to stocking a warehouse to running a business. The TCW Artificial Intelligence ETF (AIFD) is an actively managed, conviction-weighted, concentrated ETF that seeks to invest in companies that are leading the development and commercialization of artificial intelligence. We identified key sub-themes that we believe offer a strong investment opportunity as companies and consumers continue to develop and adopt AI and build the infrastructure and technology that enables it. These themes include:

  • AI Enablers, the physical infrastructure on which AI models are built, trained, and run;
  • AI Systems, the actual model and/or application of AI to a specific use case or as a general tool;
  • AI Adopters, companies that use commercial AI to disrupt their industry and create a competitive advantage for themselves.

Within these themes, the Fund seeks to invest in companies that will experience a “meaningful and measurable” effect from AI. We define meaningful in the sense that that effect will be material to fundamentals, and measurable in the sense that the market can readily see the impact. Though the Fund has high exposure to technology by its nature, this investment philosophy allows us to invest across sectors and industries. In the near term, the Fund is significantly tilted toward the infrastructure providers to take advantage of the rapid buildout of larger, smarter, more capable AI models.

Performance

The TCW Artificial Intelligence ETF was up 14.79% (Total Return) during the quarter, outperforming the S&P 500 Index return of +8.12% (Total Return). Since its inception in September 2017, AIFD has delivered a compounded annual return of 17.53% for a

cumulative return of 268.85%. This compares to the S&P 500 Index which has delivered a compounded annual return and cumulative return over the same period of 15.01% and 209.59%, respectively.

Lumentum Holdings (LITE), Arista Networks (ANET), and NVIDIA (NVDA) were the strongest performers in the quarter as the “AI trade” gained fervor among investors, broadly driven by solid earnings among the companies driving innovation in the space and various headlines that continued to corroborate the ongoing infrastructure buildout.

Lumentum shares surged through the quarter after the company reported quarterly earnings that topped expectations and previously raised guidance. The company beat top and bottom-line estimates and President and CEO Michael Hurlston said that the company saw “robust demand” across its portfolio of cloud products used in AI data centers.

Arista Networks and NVIDIA shares gained throughout the quarter as perhaps the top beneficiaries among AI infrastructure companies. In July, Trump said that he planned to reverse limitations on semiconductor sales in China that had been instituted back in April. The reversal specifically focused on H20 chips, which aren't NVIDIA's highest performing, but capable of enabling AI and generally thought to be better than any chips that China is currently able to produce. The shares continued to rise as earnings rolled in, specifically from hyperscalers, which yet again increased their spending guides, the majority of which are expected to go to NVIDIA, Arista, and other data center names. The guides continued to reinforce the bullish sentiment towards AI and the necessary infrastructure that enables it. Arista Networks, as a leading provider of networking equipment, stands to benefit from AI data center buildouts regardless of the mix of GPU vs. custom ASICs.

Trade Desk (TTD), ServiceNow (NOW), and Palo Alto Networks (PANW) were the weakest performers in the quarter.

Trade Desk shares fell following its second quarter earnings report. The company actually modestly beat expectations, however failed to match the revenue acceleration that competitors Alphabet (GOOGL) and Meta (META) showed in their respective earnings reports. Additionally, the company did not dispel concerns surrounding Amazon (AMZN) taking market share given the promising improvements it has made to its DSP (demand side platform) technology. While the report was not as promising as desired, we attribute the miss to more cyclical problems rather than structural. We believe there is a path for the company to reaccelerate growth through 2026.

ServiceNow shares were volatile in the quarter and the stock was ultimately one of our weakest performers. Software companies trended down as investors feared that artificial intelligence may start cannibalizing market share. While there is potential for AI to disrupt some existing software products, we believe ServiceNow's platform can be essential for congregating AI models in one place and streamlining them. In essence, we believe it will be enhanced by, and enhance AI, rather than be replaced by it.

Palo Alto Networks shares fell early in the quarter after news broke that the company had reached an agreement to acquire CyberArk (CYBR) (another portfolio holding) for $25 billion. The acquisition would be comprised of cash and stock and the value implies a -26% premium to CyberArk's volume weighted average in the 10 days before the story broke. As often happens, the shares of the acquirer fell while shares of the acquired company rose. We were hedged by owning both stocks and we tend to view the deal as a positive overall, we believe Palo Alto should benefit as it bolsters its portfolio of cybersecurity offerings.

Market Commentary

The three major indices (S&P 500, Dow Jones, and Nasdaq) each closed the quarter near all-time highs despite economic growth concerns, a softening labor market, elevated inflation, and tariff implementation. All sectors of the S&P 500 appreciated in the quarter except for Consumer Staples, although year-to-date through the end of September, all are in positive territory. The performance is quite impressive following the broad selloff we saw following Trump's "Liberation Day" in early April.

Through the quarter tariff-related concerns dominated headlines especially in August when President Trump signed an Executive Order raising the rate against Canadian goods to 35% from 25% and the announcement of a 90-day pause on tariff implementation with Mexico after making agreements with many trade partners including Japan, U.K., Vietnam, Indonesia, and the Philippines. As we rolled into the end of the summer, tariff headlines have all but been forgotten, save for lone headlines of notable developments or forward-looking mentions of potential inflation.

Both growth and inflation for the second quarter came in higher than expected suggesting a resilient U.S. economy and consumer that has thus far weathered the impact from higher tariffs. After a mild contraction in 1Q, the latest 2Q GDP read came in at 3.8% led by strong consumer spending and a noticeable decline in imports due in no small part to the implementation and/or increase in tariffs.

As anticipated, the Fed delivered a 25-basis point rate cut and signaled potential for two more cuts by year-end sending major indices, including the S&P 500, NASDAQ, and Russell 2000, to new all-time highs. Meanwhile, the re-emergence of tariff threats and the reality of a government shutdown, served as reminders of headline risk, even if investors treated them more as background noise than as drivers of fundamentals.

Looking forward, we expect the equity market to remain data dependent, with investor attention increasingly focused on whether earnings growth in 2026 can sustain current valuation levels. At roughly 23x forward earnings, multiples remain elevated by historical standards and leave little margin for disappointment. To justify these valuations, profit growth and free cash flow delivery will need to do more of the heavy lifting, particularly in a slower growth environment. There are many constructive dynamics supporting the equity market, including generally healthy corporate balance sheets, ample liquidity, and powerful secular growth drivers such as artificial intelligence, digital infrastructure, and the energy transition. At the same time, we are mindful of risks, including policy uncertainty, tariff disputes, and the potential for fiscal disruptions, all of which could unsettle sentiment at the margin. Against this backdrop, our focus remains on companies with durable business models, recurring free cash flow, and disciplined capital allocation, as well as those positioned to benefit from the earnings growth prospects associated with long-duration secular themes. In our view, these characteristics offer the best potential for compounding value in an environment where earnings delivery, rather than further multiple expansion, is likely to be the key driver of equity returns.

Recent headlines regarding a potential AI bubble are, in our view, misguided. With the Mag 7 (ex-TSLA (TSLA)) currently trading around 30x forward earnings, the multiple premium to the market is justified by significantly higher growth rates. In fact, the PEG ratio for the AI group of approximately 1.6x compares very favorably to the broader market index at slightly over 2.0x. More importantly, new capacity in the cloud for running AI models is being absorbed as soon as it is turned on and all major hyperscalers have pointed to a supply/demand imbalance that is expected to last well into 2026. Yes, AI stocks have had a significant run over the past 2+ years, but absolute valuation doesn't approach prior bubble levels, supply is being consumed faster than it can be created, and the amount of infrastructure the industry will need to build out to serve billions of users interacting with AI hundreds of times per day—not to mention the eventual overlay of physical AI—will dwarf what we have spent to date. There is a big difference between “bubble” and “overvalued”. We see no evidence of a bubble and would argue that many of the most prominent AI names are actually undervalued. However, this notion does seem to be the current wall of worry in front of investors and 3Q25 earnings season is the best chance for companies to reinforce the strong fundamental outlook.

data.title: M&A Momentum: A Hidden Bullish Indicator Heading Into 2026?
data.description:
Bankers and underwriters have plenty to be thankful for. Bond issuance is running hot, this year’s IPO count is up (at least it was before the government shutdown), and interest rate volatility has cooled - potentially paving the way for more corporate dealmaking as we head into 2026. Momentum continues to build on that last front.

According to Wall Street Horizon’s coverage universe of 11,000 global equities, the third quarter of 2025 marked the highest number of M&A closes since Q4 2024. On a rolling four-quarter total, the count is the highest going back to early 2023.

A solid finish to the year could take us back to 2022 levels - a period that benefited from the 2021 deal boom. The chart of M&A announcements shows a similar recovery.

A Deal Deluge: Global M&A Activity on the Rise in 2025

Source: Wall Street Horizon

North America appears to be at the heart of the renaissance. BofA Global Research pointed out that North American M&A volumes (on a dollar basis) summed to $348 billion in October. While that was down from $421 billion in September, the six-month average notched the best level since October 2014.1 Zooming in further geographically, Goldman Sachs’ team recently estimated that announced U.S. M&A volumes were 35% higher on a year-over-year basis as of early November.2

Is uncertainty high? Yes. Is the macroeconomic foundation shaky? Yes. Are companies seeking creative solutions? It appears so.

What’s more, our team dug into the actual transactions (both proposed and finalized). The main message is that M&A is happening across sectors and industries. Health Care collabs are among the most numerous, but we see consolidations in cyclical areas like Financials, Consumer Discretionary, and Materials. Tech is also in the limelight, with an increasing number of firms tapping the bond market to fund AI capex and M&A.

Here are some of the standout deals, and why they matter for investors attempting to get a handle on the state of CEO confidence beyond earnings reports and investor conferences.

Health Care

Pfizer → Metsera

The GLP-1 weight-loss arms race ratcheted up when Pfizer (PFE) acquired Metsera (MTSR). It was quite the saga, and the move strengthens Pfizer’s position in a niche that rival Eli Lilly (LLY) has come to dominate. With Metsera’s assets and pipeline, Pfizer may simply be aiming to diversify its revenue base, which has leaned heavily on its COVID vaccine in recent years. PFE’s fat dividend and slow price climb are welcomed by many of its income-seeking investors.

Merck → Cidara Therapeutics

Also in Health Care, Merck’s (MRK) purchase of Cidara Therapeutics (CDTX) adds a long-acting antiviral platform and potential blockbuster flu treatment to its infectious disease portfolio. The $9.2 billion acquisition offers optionality and reinforces Pfizer’s vast oncology franchise.

Consumer Staples

Kimberly-Clark → Kenvue

Earlier this month, a major deal crossed the wires between a household name and a recent spinoff entity. Kimberly-Clark (KMB) announced it would buy Kenvue (KVUE) in a $49 billion cash-and-stock deal. Recall that the acquired company had been under intense pressure from the Trump administration regarding Tylenol’s alleged health effects on pregnant women.

Most experts assert the over-the-counter pain-relief medication is safe, but the story weighed on KVUE shares. KMB is willing to take on the risk. Investors were not enthused, though, as shares plunged from near $120 to under $100 (for a time) after the news broke. The KMB bulls need a Band-Aid.

Financials

Huntington Bancshares → Cadence Bancorporation

Pivoting to more economically sensitive areas, recent M&A activity within the Regional Banks industry is encouraging for the macro bulls. They say that banks are sold, not bought (meaning acquisitions are more common amid market stress), but when Huntington Bancshares (HBAN) confirmed its intention to purchase Cadence Bank (CADE) for $7.4 billion, it was seen as a positive. The acquisition strengthens HBAN’s southern U.S. footprint.

Fifth Third Bancorp → Comerica

Another regional banking deal landed early in Q4. Fifth Third Bank (FITB) announced it would buy Comerica (CMA) in a $10.9 billion all-stock deal. Assuming the acquisition is completed, it would be one of the largest regional bank mergers in years, creating a more geographically diversified FITB with a significant presence across the Midwest and Texas.

Charles Schwab → Forge Global

On the brokerage side, Charles Schwab (SCHW) is investing in Forge Global (FRGE). The purchase provides the $168 billion market cap financial services firm with direct access to private-market trading infrastructure, an area gaining traction as more late-stage companies remain private for longer. Forge also specializes in secondary trading of private shares, potentially laying the groundwork for Schwab to offer its clients earlier access to early-stage companies.

Consumer Discretionary

TriArtisan Capital / Treville Capital / Yadav Enterprises → Denny’s

Schwab investors may be pleased with returns across stocks and bonds so far this year, but it’s clear that there’s angst among many consumer cohorts. Lower- and middle-income households struggle as the “K-shaped” economy gets increasing attention. In a private-equity-led buyout, Denny’s (DENN) will likely undergo restructuring. The restaurant company has struggled with traffic and modernization, making a private-equity turnaround appealing. Will it turn out to be a grand slam? Time will tell.

Materials

Coeur Mining → New Gold

Wrapping up with a glittering slice of the global commodity market: gold. On November 3, Coeur Mining (CDE) announced plans to buy New Gold (NGD) in an all-stock transaction. The deal offers Coeur a potential operational advantage and boosts its reserves at a time when gold prices remain high, near $4,000 per troy ounce, on geopolitical and macro uncertainty.

Gold miners more broadly boast stronger balance sheets than previous cycles, and with higher free cash flow (given gold prices), executives in the industry have plenty of capital-deployment options.

The Bottom Line

These are just a handful of many recently announced M&A transactions. Could it be a bullish signal as year-end approaches? Perhaps. CEOs and CIOs are venturing out on the risk curve for growth beyond organic means. Surely not all of the deals will work out to a T, but after many quarters of quiet M&A volumes, activity has turned up meaningfully lately.

1 BofA Global Research, BofA Securities, November 18, 20252 Goldman Sachs Insights, Goldman Sachs, November 18, 2025

Original Post

data.title: DIVZ: A Defensive Dividend ETF
data.description:
The Opal Dividend Income ETF (DIVZ) is an option for investors who want to play defense. It holds a concentrated portfolio of high-dividend companies with low beta and attractive valuations. Next is a closer look at DIVZ’s sector allocation, individual holdings, valuation profile, performance, and tradability relative to the S&P 500 and its most direct peers.

ETF Description & Highlights

DIVZ aims to deliver higher dividend yields than the S&P 500 while keeping volatility lower than the broader market. It is an actively managed fund that, without tracking any particular index, has a selective approach to building a concentrated portfolio of 25 to 35 U.S. companies. The portfolio construction starts with an initial universe of companies with a market cap of over $8 billion, which sits at the higher end of a typical definition of mid-caps ($2 to $10 billion). From this pool, the fund’s advisor screens companies for cash flow strength and competitive advantages. As a next step, the advisor selects companies trading below their fair value, employing multiples such as price-to-earnings, market cap-to-book value, and free cash flow yield, while also favoring those with above-average free cash flow.

As of November 12, 2025, DIVZ had $206.3 million in assets under management, holding a concentrated portfolio of 30 companies with an average market capitalization of $105.45 billion. Compared with the S&P 500, its primary benchmark and represented by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), which carries an average market cap of $462.68 billion, DIVZ has a market cap allocation with greater concentration in large- and mid-caps, which account for 52.4% and 35.6% of the portfolio, respectively, while mega caps make up only 10.6%, as opposed to 45.5% in the S&P 500.

The fund’s top ten holdings bring together a group of high-dividend names across energy, utilities, financial services, and defensive consumer sectors. MPLX (5.42%) and Enbridge (4.77%) anchor the energy allocation. Utilities show up right behind them via NextEra Energy (5.41%) and Dominion Energy (3.86%). The fund’s financial exposure includes Citigroup (4.79%), Goldman Sachs (4.22%), and CME Group (4.13%), while Verizon (5.22%), Philip Morris (5.17%), and Johnson & Johnson (3.83%) represent mature and stable businesses. Together, these top holdings illustrate the fund’s focus on companies where dividends stem from relatively stable cash flows.

The table below brings together a handful of dividend funds that will serve as the peer group for DIVZ in this analysis. At the top of the list is the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (VYM), the largest fund in the group. Its market-cap-weighted design follows a broadly diversified index that screens for above-average dividend payers. Next comes the SPDR S&P Dividend ETF (SDY), which takes a more focused path, tracking a yield-weighted index that requires companies to have increased dividends for at least 20 consecutive years. The third fund, Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), blends quality, profitability, and income into a rules-based strategy but also follows a market-cap-weighted structure. The next three funds incorporate low-volatility strategies into their dividend core theme, similar to DIVZ. The Invesco S&P 500 High Dividend Low Volatility ETF (SPHD) identifies the least volatile names from a list of the S&P 500’s highest-yielding stocks with a yield-based weighting system. The next peer, FlexShares Quality Dividend Defensive Index Fund ETF (QDEF), follows a factor-oriented strategy tied to quality and yields, while VictoryShares US Large Cap High Dividend Volatility Weighted ETF (CDL) employs a more statistical method, tracking an index where each constituent’s weight is driven by its historical volatility.

DIVZ’s largest allocation is to utilities at 19.7% of the portfolio, followed by a cluster of income-oriented exposures, including consumer staples at 16.9%, financial services at 15.9%, energy at 15.2%, and healthcare at 15.0%. Further down the list, allocations drop quite a bit, with communication services at 5.3%, consumer discretionary at 3.3%, technology at 3.2%, basic materials at 3.0%, and industrials at 2.7%. Given this sector mix, DIVZ becomes most overweight in utilities (+17.3%), energy (+12.2%), and consumer staples (+12.1%) relative to the S&P 500, offsetting a heavy underweight position in technology (-32.7%), with still significant gaps in consumer discretionary (-7.2%) and communication services (-5.0%).

Breaking down DIVZ’s allocation by sector, we can see that holdings in utilities are fairly concentrated, with just five holdings. The fund leans on electric utilities, with the renewables-focused NextEra Energy and American Electric Power, a large, regulated grid operator in the Midwest and South, among the largest holdings. The sector exposure is complemented by Dominion Energy, an integrated electric and gas utility; NRG Energy, a merchant generation and retail power company; and the gas utility Brookfield Infrastructure Corp.

The consumer staples exposure is somewhat more diversified in terms of sub-sectors, though with only five core holdings. In tobacco, DIVZ owns Philip Morris International, the sector's largest position, and Altria Group. The fund owns PepsiCo, adding exposure to the soft drinks space, while Hershey represents the packaged foods industry, and Procter & Gamble adds exposure to household products.

In financial services, the fund again takes a concentrated approach, with four holdings across banks, capital markets, and insurance. DIVZ owns Citigroup and CME Group, the derivatives exchange operator, while the exposure to investment banking comes through Goldman Sachs Group, and American International Group broadens the sector coverage to multiline insurers.

Outside the top three sectors, DIVZ still has meaningful exposure to several large industries. For instance, in pharmaceuticals, the fund has stakes in Johnson & Johnson and AbbVie, while holdings in managed healthcare include Cigna and UnitedHealth. It also has significant positions in telecommunications services via Verizon Communications and technology through Accenture, comprising a mix of mature operations, contrasting with the largest companies in these two sectors, such as NVIDIA, Microsoft, and Alphabet, all still growth players, despite already well-established operations.

DIVZ’s defensive tilt is also clear relative to dividend peers. The largest overweights versus the peer average are in utilities (+8.8%) and energy (+5.8%). Those gaps are magnified by funds such as SCHD and QDEF, which keep utilities at minimal levels (0.1% and 4.6%), and by QDEF and VYM in energy, where allocations are 2.4% and 8.7%, respectively. It is fair to say, though, that while it is expected to support income stability, it also means more sensitivity to oil prices’ moves and regulatory headlines in the utilities sector. On the underweight side, DIVZ’s modest exposure to technology and industrials leads to allocation gaps of -8.8% and -5.8%, respectively, compared to peers. Key contributors to these gaps are QDEF (34.1%) and VYM (16.4%) in technology and SDY (18.1%) in industrials.

Despite these differences, there are pockets of alignment. Consumer staples exposure is broadly consistent across DIVZ, SDY, SCHD, and SPHD, all clustering in the mid- to high-teens. As a result, looking across individual peers, DIVZ’s profile is close to CDL, with a high sector correlation of 0.94. On the other hand, correlations with VYM, SDY, SCHD, and SPHD are moderate in the 0.37-0.43 range, reflecting VYM, SDY, and SCHD’s greater bias toward industrials and technology, while SPHD has a strong focus on real estate. QDEF is the clear outlier, with a negative correlation (-0.14) driven by its very large technology allocation (34.1%) and relatively low exposure to energy and utilities. Overall, DIVZ’s correlation with peers is moderate, especially compared to other categories such as growth funds, where we see a much stronger sector consensus in general.

Dividend Yields in Line With Peers

With a dividend yield of 2.7%, DIVZ offers competitive yields compared to large peers like VYM (2.5%) and SDY (2.6%), well ahead of the S&P 500’s 1.1%, which is the fund’s primary goal. However, it falls short of SCHD and SPHD, both at 3.8%, and also CDL’s 3.3%. DIVZ’s dividend growth is not the best either, as peers have managed to grow dividends at low- to mid-single-digit over the past years, unlike DIVZ’s negative growth.

Attractive Valuation Relative to the S&P 500

At a 14.3x P/E, DIVZ trades at a substantial 37% discount to the S&P 500’s 22.6x multiple. This valuation gap is spread across most sectors, with pronounced discounts in areas like consumer discretionary, communication services, industrials, and technology, where DIVZ’s holdings trade at an average discount of at least 50% to the S&P 500. A second driver comes from DIVZ’s sector mix, given the fund's tilt to lower-multiple areas, such as utilities and consumer staples, along with underweight positions in expensive sectors, especially technology. Individual holdings reinforce the valuation profile. Overweight positions in Verizon (9.3x P/E), Citigroup (12.7x), Cigna (10.2x), and AIG (12.3x) are core contributors to the fund’s low valuation.

From a growth perspective, DIVZ is clearly on the conservative side relative to the S&P 500. Its historical earnings growth of 2.8% is far below the S&P 500’s 9.5%, and its sales growth of 7.1% also trails the benchmark’s 8.0% pace. This weaker growth profile is largely linked to the fund’s sector stance, with a heavy overweight in slower-growth areas such as utilities, consumer staples, and financial services. In addition, positions across all sectors exhibit more modest metrics compared to the benchmark. Holdings such as Citigroup, Dominion Energy, and Accenture are good examples of the fund’s profile.

EBITDA and ROE figures tell a nuanced profitability story. On one hand, the fund’s EBITDA margin of 35.8% is 0.4 percentage points above the S&P 500’s 35.4%, as higher margins across several key sectors, including consumer staples, financial services, and utilities, offset lower exposure to technology, a high-margin sector. The flip side is return on equity. DIVZ’s ROE of 18.7% is far below the S&P 500’s 63.4%, reflecting a portfolio that shows consistently lower ROEs across all sectors, driven by overweight positions in names like NextEra, Citigroup, Dominion Energy, CME Group, Verizon, Cigna, and Chevron, many of which show lower or even negative ROE.

DIVZ’s P/E sits marginally below the peer group average (15.0x). QDEF (17.8x) has by far the most expensive valuations, reflecting a heavy 34.1% allocation to technology. In contrast, SCHD, SPHD, and CDL sit at the lower end of the valuation range at about 13x P/E, given a sector mix that is overweight energy, a value sector, but has light exposure to growth areas like technology. That said, it is not surprising that their historical earnings growth is the lowest in the group. Thus, DIVZ’s valuation seems to reflect a defensive but still measured approach, which is consistent with its earnings growth, which is the second-highest among peers.

Performance Highlights a Conservative Profile

With its inception in 2021, DIVZ has not completed five years of trading activity yet. Although this is still a relatively new fund, its track record indicates that it has delivered on its defensive approach, holding up much better in the bearish year of 2022, when it gained 3.5%, while the S&P 500 declined 18.2%, and only SPHD also stayed in the positive territory (+0.6%). Despite that, DIVZ’s 3-year annualized return of 10.4% has not kept up with the S&P 500’s 21.0% during a predominantly bullish environment but has outpaced most peers, logging the third-highest gain, only behind VYM (+12.0%) and QDEF (+18.0%).

DIVZ’s volatility has been the lowest among peers, with a 3-year beta of 0.58, while other dividend funds have been in the 0.68-0.81 range. Anyway, it is fair to say that the whole peer group has seen much more contained volatility than the S&P 500 (beta 1.00), particularly SPHD (0.68) and CDL (0.71), reflecting their low volatility mandates as well. DIVZ’s moderate price action is also illustrated through its capture ratios, with 3-year readings of 53 (upside) and 67 (downside) pointing to much smoother price action than all peers.

DIVZ’s liquidity metrics are relatively modest, with an average daily trading volume of $1.20 million, well below large dividend peers such as VYM ($204 million) and SCHD ($459 million). This lower liquidity is reflected in a 0.08% bid-ask spread, roughly double the peer group average. DIVZ’s 80% turnover rate is the highest in the group, driving its expense ratio up to 0.65%, also well above peers, which range from 0.06% to 0.37%, and becoming a cost headwind for DIVZ. The fund’s tracking error in the 10-11% range is in the middle of the pack, above VYM, SDY, and QDEF, highlighting a more concentrated, actively managed approach. Finally, DIVZ’s 3-year Sharpe ratio of 0.45 stands well below those of top-performing peers like QDEF (1.18) and VYM (0.67), indicating that the fund’s higher turnover and modest volatility have not translated into superior risk-adjusted results.

Final Thoughts

Blending cash flow, valuations, and competitive factors in the selection process, DIVZ leans heavily into mature and cash-generative businesses in sectors such as utilities, energy, and consumer staples, but away from growth segments. This helps explain a clear discount to the S&P 500 and a lower beta profile, one of the fund’s core goals, while dividend yields are at 2.7%, below top-tier levels. In a market that is waiting for economic data to update growth expectations, amid questions about rich stock valuations, especially in technology, and a renewed interest in low-volatility and income strategies, DIVZ’s conservative positioning looks attractive now, even if it does not lead to outperformance.

That said, the case for DIVZ rests on how much weight an investor puts on capital preservation versus total return potential, as the fund’s sector mix and core holdings suggest it may continue to hold up better during selloffs, which could be helpful if growth cools into 2026, leading to increased volatility. The tradeoff is its modest growth profile, which I expect will continue to affect its long-term performance. Therefore, I take a neutral view on this fund, as investors looking for income and still keeping a more significant exposure to growth will likely find better options elsewhere in the peer group.

data.title: Gabelli GDL Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Elevated M&A volume activity continued in the third quarter with $1.3 trillion in announced transactions. This follows $1.1 trillion in the first quarter and $1.06 trillion in the second quarter. The total volume of $3.4 trillion in the first 9 months represents a 32% increase relative to the same period in 2024. However, the number of deals dropped to 8,912, the worst third quarter in 20 years.

The quarter was led by megadeals. One of the largest deals in recent years was Union Pacific's $85 billion acquisition of Norfolk Southern. The largest private transaction in history was announced during the quarter: the $55 billion acquisition of Electronic Arts by PIE, Silver Lake, and Affinity Partners. Other notable deals were Anglo American's $17 billion merger of equals with Teck and Palo Alto Networks' $25 billion acquisition of CyberArk.

Technology and financials continued to be the top sectors for M&A. These two sectors accounted for over $1 trillion in deal activity during the first nine months of 2025. Private equity-backed transactions, as well as cross-border transactions, kept up their recent pace.

The regulatory environment has continued to be viewed favorably by market participants. The antitrust agencies have shown a willingness to accept remedies and divestitures when appropriate, enabling more transactions to proceed. This evolving approach has begun to restore corporate confidence in the regulatory process, and is expected to support continued strength in new deal activity. The rate-cutting cycle started by the Federal Reserve in September should also lead to lower financing costs for acquisitions.

However, several macro factors, including geopolitical tensions and economic uncertainty, remain unresolved. However, the expectation is that the several months of a pickup in M&A activity since the spring will continue, driven by strategic imperatives such as the need to compete globally in the face of shifting tariff dynamics, access to new technologies, and entry into emerging growth verticals.

Our top contributors to performance for the quarter (based upon price change and position size) were TEGNA (+0.49%), WideOpenWest (+0.31%), Verona Pharma (+0.31%), Lennar Class B (LEN.B) (+0.28%), and Kellanova (K) (+0.27%). Our top detractors were Cantaloupe (CFLP) (-0.13%), Covestro (-0.04%), Manchester United (MANU) (-0.03%), James Hardie (JHX) (-0.02%), and Schlumberger (SLB) (-0.02%).

Deals in The Pipeline

Verona Pharma (VRNA - $106.71 - NASDAQ) is a biopharma company focused on developing and commercialization of therapies for treatment of chronic respiratory diseases. Merck agreed to acquire VRNA for $107 per share in cash on July 9. Shareholder approval and U.S. antitrust approval have already been received. The deal remains subject to UK court approval and is expected to close in early fourth quarter of 2025.

TEGNA (TGNA - $20.33 - NYSE) is TV broadcasting company with 64 stations. TEGNA was unsuccessful selling the company to Standard General in 2023 due to delays in obtaining FCC approval. On August 19, Nexstar Media agreed to acquire TEGNA for $22.00 per share in cash. The transaction remains subject to shareholder approval and regulatory approvals. It is expected to be completed by the second half of 2026.

Closed Deals

On January 30, the Justice Department sued to block the transaction, but on June 27, a settlement was reached. The deal closed on July 2, 2025.

SpringWorks Therapeutics is a biotech company focused on treatment of various forms of cancer and other rare diseases, particularly ovarian and endometrial tumors and cancers. On April 28, 2025, the company agreed to be acquired by Germany-based Merck KGaA for $47.00 per share in cash. Investment Objective

The Fund is a diversified, closed-end management investment company. The Fund's investment objective is to achieve absolute returns in various market conditions without excessive risk of capital. data.title: New Jersey Municipal Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
• The fund posted returns of 3.30% (Institutional shares) and 3.24% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the third quarter of 2025.
  • The fund's outperformance of its benchmark was primarily driven by its long duration (high interest rate sensitivity) and long yield curve positioning. An exposure to positively convex (the rate at which duration changes in response to interest rate movements) discount structure bonds also benefited performance.
  • The fund had an overweight allocation to bonds maturing in 15 or more years, while it was fully invested, which meant that the cash reserve was limited.

Contributors

The tax-backed local, health, education, housing, and school district sectors were large contributors to relative performance. From a ratings perspective, investment grade bonds were a significantly larger weighting than high yield securities and therefore garnered a larger share of the active results. The fund's long yield curve exposure was additive, specifically bonds maturing in 15 or more years. Income from a fully invested posture and low cash reserves contributed to total returns.

Detractors

Underweight holdings in BB and B rated securities produced modestly negative active returns. A greater portion of the fund's high yield exposure is in non-rated credits, which performed positively during the quarter. An underweight position in 5% coupon bonds weighed on relative returns, though this was offset by the allocation to positively convex discount bonds.

Further insight

The fund extended duration in the spring amid market volatility to lock in attractive yields, capitalizing on a steepening municipal bond yield curve relative to U.S. Treasuries. While the fund was early to position long, our conviction and patience were rewarded late in the quarter as rates rallied following the Federal Reserve's signal of a potential interest rate cut. Market participants began to recognize the compelling value of the municipal bond asset class, both in relative terms versus other fixed income sectors and in absolute terms based on historical valuation metrics. This strategic positioning helped the fund to navigate uncertainty and boost performance as sentiment shifted.

data.title: Barings BDC: Q3 Earnings Show Strong Dividend Coverage
data.description:
Overview

As business development companies continue to face headwinds, there are still some more resilient choices throughout the sector. I believe that Barings BDC (BBDC) is one of the better choices in the sector right now, despite some of its challenges. When I previously covered BBDC, I issued a hold rating due to the healthy dividend coverage but limited growth potential. Now that BBDC has reported its Q3 earnings, I wanted to revisit the performance and assess its strengths and weaknesses for the remainder of the year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that BBDC's share price has declined by about 10.75%. BBDC has essentially failed to participate in the market rally over the last few quarters, which is likely attributed to the growing concerns around the debt market. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return sits around 1.75% over the same time frame. BBDC now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 11.6%, which makes it an enticing choice for investors seeking a high-income generation.

Based on the latest earnings, BBDC has one of the strongest distribution coverage rates throughout the sector. As a result, there's a strong likelihood that BBDC will be able to avoid any large distribution cuts in the near term. Additionally, the portfolio has demonstrated growth in some key areas, such as net investment income and a reduction in operating expenses. However, the NAV continues to trend downward, which indicates there is still some room for improvement. So let's start by taking a look at the most recent earnings report to assess its performance over the quarter.

Q3 Earnings

According to the latest portfolio breakdown, BBDC has total investments at fair value of $2.04B. In order to generate its earnings, BBDC's portfolio contains a diverse range of debt investments. Unlike traditional equities, BBDC doesn't rely on the earnings growth of its positions to grow its portfolio. Instead, BBDC relies on a borrower's ability to keep up and repay its debt maintenance, which is an easier bet to make. The portfolio is most heavily allocated to senior secured first lien debt, which sits at the top of the corporate capital structure and has the highest priority for repayment.

This structure is ideal because it protects BBDC from a scenario where a borrower is materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their debt payments. If a borrower defaults and needs to liquidate assets, BBDC would be the first in line to get repaid before other forms of debt. The portfolio's breakdown by investment type is as follows:

  • First Lien: 70%
  • Equity: 17%
  • Second Lien: 5%
  • Joint Venture: 4%
  • Mezzanine: 3%
  • Structured: 1%

Expanding on this diversity, BBDC allocates its assets across a wide variety of industries. As we can see below, BBDC is most heavily allocated to Services: Business investments at 18% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Banking & Finance and High Tech Industries, which accounts for 17% and 10% of assets, respectively. So far it seems like BBDC's allocation keeps the portfolio's exposure to tariff-impacted industries limited.

BBDC recently reported its Q3 earnings, and the results were a bit mixed. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.32 per share. Total investment income amounted to $72.4M, which was an improvement from the prior year's total investment income of $70.8M. Although total interest income declined to $48.8M, the increase in total investment income can be attributed to higher dividend income received during the quarter.

Additionally, operating expenses decreased to $37.4M for the quarter from its prior year's operating expenses of $39.6M. Interest expenses and incentive management fees have both decreased year over year. Therefore, the only increase was seen in BBDC's base management fees, which now total $8.4M. Unfortunately, BBDC continues its streak of net realized losses, which has amounted to $1.3M for Q3. This was accompanied by another $8.7M decrease in net realized depreciation, which results in a net increase in operations of $23.5M for the quarter

From a balance sheet perspective, BBDC has remained solid. For instance, cash and foreign currencies amount to $83.2M, which is a significant improvement from the prior year's cash balance of $65.9M. However, total borrowings under the credit facility have increased to $353.9M, which is also a sizeable increase from the prior year's balance of $347.8M. As a result, BBDC's total net assets have slightly declined to $1.16B from its prior year's total assets of $1.19B.

Additionally, BBDC has a healthy debt maturity ladder, as seen below. There is a small amount of private placement loans maturing this year, while there are a little over $400M in debt maturing in 2026. However, most of BBDC's debts aren't maturing until 2029.

Valuation & Risk Profile

At the time of my last coverage, BBDC traded at a discount to NAV of 13.5%. Following the recent price decline, BBDC now trades at a larger discount to NAV of approximately 19.46%. For reference, BBDC has traded at an average discount to NAV of 15.89% over the last five years. Despite BBDC trading at a larger discount to the NAV price level, this doesn't automatically mean that it's a good time to accumulate shares. In fact, the larger discount to NAV can be a direct indication of the challenges that the portfolio faces at this time.

Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $9.95 per share, which indicates a potential upside of about 12% from the current level. However, I believe that the upside growth is ultimately limited while interest rates remain elevated. The higher cost of debt can create thinner operating margins and reduce the number of new investment opportunities that are available for BBDC. For instance, total originations during the quarter were $149M, but this was offset by sales and repayments of $230M, which led to a net decline in assets for the quarter. This has caused BBDC to maintain its NAV decline over the last twelve months. For reference, here are the quarter end NAV amounts:

  • Q3 2024: $11.32 per share
  • Q4 2024: $11.29 per share
  • Q1 2025: $11.29 per share
  • Q2 2025: $11.18 per share
  • Q3 2025: $11.10 per share

So even though the Fed has initiated its second 25 basis point cut for 2025, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. While higher interest rates can be great for income generation, they also put pressure on borrowers by lowering their operating margins. As long as the cost of debt remains high, I anticipate that there will be fewer borrowers in the market that BBDC can capitalize on.

Despite the higher interest rate environment, BBDC has maintained a relatively lower level of non-accruals. Non-accruals represent the amount of borrowers that are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance. As a result, these borrowers aren't actively contributing towards BBDC's earnings. According to the latest earnings call, management confirmed that non-accruals amounted to 0.4% of assets.

During the quarter, we removed one asset from non-accrual status that was restructured and moved one asset on to non-accruals that is covered by the Sierra CSA. As our internal marks on Sierra accounts remain below the CSA support amount, any prospective losses at the current marks will offset upon settlement of the CSA. We remain confident in the credit quality of the underlying portfolio. We expect BBDC's differentiated reach and scale, coupled with its core focus on middle market credit and unmatched alignment with shareholders to provide positive outcomes in the quarters and years to come. - Matthew Freund, President

A caveat to note is that the 'Sierra CSA' refers to the credit support agreement from a merger than took place in 2021. This merger protects BBDC from the losses on the acquired Sierra portfolio. Just as a reference point, here are some of the relevant non-accrual rates for some of the more notable BDCs in the sector:

  • Hercules Capital (HTGC): 1.1% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • Ares Capital (ARCC): 1% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • FS KKR Capital (FSK): 2.9% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.26 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.6%. As previously mentioned, net investment income amounted to $0.32 per share for the quarter. This indicates that BBDC's earnings support the distribution at a coverage rate of ~123%. This is a great improvement from the coverage rate of 107.7% at the time of my last coverage. Therefore, BBDC remains a solid choice for investors seeking income stability, despite some of the challenges that it faces.

Looking back on the payout history below, we can see that BBDC has been able to consistently increase payouts since the bottom of its range during 2020. Although the coverage remains healthy, I do not anticipate any dividend raises in the near term, as long as the NAV continues to trend downward. However, management did finally confirm that they have $0.65 per share worth of spillover income available. Therefore, there's a good chance that there will be some supplement distributions announced over the next twelve months. This is a great position for the dividend to be in as we transition into the 2026 period.

However, it's worth mentioning that the distributions received from BBDC do not offer any tax efficiency. Since the payouts are being supported by interest income from its portfolio of borrowers, the distributions are classified as ordinary dividends, which have the greatest tax burden for investors. So if you're an investor that wants to limit the overall tax liability experienced within your portfolio, a position in BBDC would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on BBDC due to the consistent NAV declines. Although BBDC has demonstrated some healthy fundamentals, such as a strong dividend coverage, consistent net investment income, and low non-accruals, the portfolio is still facing challenges that limit the appeal. For instance, management wasn't able to allocate a meaningful amount of capital towards new investment activity for the quarter, which resulted in a net decrease in assets. So while BBDC is great for income investors, I believe the upside is limited as long as interest rates remain elevated.
